coredata.dc:title: Purification of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots and its application in polymer solar cells
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Yanyun
Dai, Chunai
Hao, Changshi
Guo, Hao
Yan, Luting
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) have potential application in photovoltaic devices because of their adjustable bandgap and remarkable dispersion in amphiphilic systems. In this study, N-GQDs were synthesized and purified, and changes in their optical properties before and after purification were compared. The introduction of the N-GQDs into the ZnO electron transport layer (ETL) of a bulk heterojunction polymer solar cell based on PTB7:PC71BM enhanced the power conversion efficiency (PCE) significantly. Purified N-GQDs showed stronger N doping, obvious blue shift of photoluminescence (PL) emission, and improved dispersion in organic system. After chloroform extraction and purification, the N-GQDs derived from citric acid-urea ethanol solution had the least loss and the most prominent optical performance (quantum yield =41.95%). The purified N-GQDs showed longer fluorescence lifetime than that before purification and still showed strong PL performance after compounding with ZnO ETL. N-GQDs modified ZnO ETL layer has stronger light transmittance and lower impedance. The stronger light transmittance is capable of improving the light absorption of the active layer while the lower impedance can promote electron transfer. Both properties are conductive to the PCE.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots
Purification
Modification
Electron transport layer
Polymer solar cell

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Modification of ZnO ETL by N-GQDs and photovoltaic device fabrication
Effect of purification on the morphology and size of N-GQDs
Effect of purification on the optical properties of N-GQDs
Effect of purification on photovoltaic performance

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image of purified N-GQDs (a: A-GQDs-DL, b: A-GQDs-SE, c: Ae-GQDs-SE, d: U-GQDs-DL, e:U-GQDs -SE, f: Ue-GQDs-SE). The illustrations are the corresponding particle size distributions of N-GQDs.
a) FTIR spectra and b) XRD patterns of A-GQDs-SE, Ae-GQDs-SE, U-GQDs-SE and Ue-GQDs-SE.
(a) (b) High-resolution fitting XPS spectra of C 1 s and N 1 s peaks of U-GQDs and U-GQDs-DL; (c) (d) High-resolution fitting XPS spectra of C 1 s and N 1 s peaks of Ue-GQDs and Ue-GQDs-SE.

(a) PL spectra of A-GQDs, A-GQDs-SE, Ae-GQDs, Ae-GQDs-Os and Ae-GQDs-SE at 365 nm excitation wavelength (b) PL spectra of U-GQDs, U-GQDs-SE, Ue-GQDs, Ue-GQDs-Os and Ue-GQDs-SE at excitation wavelength of 365 nm.
UV-Vis, PLE and PL spectra at different excitation wavelengths (A-GQDs, A-GQDs-SE, U-GQDs, U-GQDs-SE, Ae-GQDs, Ae-GQDs-SE, Ae-GQDs-Os, Ue-GQDs, Ue -GQDs-SE and Ue-GQDs-Os).
(a) and (b) are UV-Vis absorption spectra and PL spectra of A-GQDs, A-GQDs-SE, U-GQDs, U-GQDs-SE, Ae-GQDs, Ae-GQDs-SE, Ae-GQDs-Os, Ue-GQDs, Ue-GQDs-SE and Ue-GQDs-Os, respectively.
Time resolved PL decay curves of (a) A-GQDs, A-GQDs-SE, Ae-GQDs, Ae-GQDs-SE, Ae-GQDs-Os and (b) U-GQDs, U-GQDs-SE, Ue-GQDs, Ue-GQDs-SE and Ue-GQDs-Os.
EQE of Ae-GQDs-Os and Ae-GQDs-SE modified photovoltaic devices.
Elemental compositions analysis of U-GQDs, U-GQDs-DL, Ue-GQDs和Ue-GQDs-SE.
XPS data analysis of C 1 s spectra of U-GQDs, U-GQDs-DL, Ue-GQDs和Ue-GQDs-SE.
XPS data analysis of N 1 s spectra of U-GQDs、U-GQDs-DL、Ue-GQDs和Ue-GQDs-SE.
QY comparison of N-GQDs before and after purification.
Photovoltaic parameters of N-GQDs modified devices.

head.ce:title: Purification of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots and its application in polymer solar cells

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of materials science and Engineering, School of physical science and Engineering, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of materials science and Engineering, School of physical science and Engineering, Beijing Jiaotong University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of materials science and Engineering, School of physical science and Engineering, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing, 100044

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: School of physical science and Engineering, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of physical science and Engineering, Beijing Jiaotong University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) have potential application in photovoltaic devices because of their adjustable bandgap and remarkable dispersion in amphiphilic systems. In this study, N-GQDs were synthesized and purified, and changes in their optical properties before and after purification were compared. The introduction of the N-GQDs into the ZnO electron transport layer (ETL) of a bulk heterojunction polymer solar cell based on PTB7:PC71BM enhanced the power conversion efficiency (PCE) significantly. Purified N-GQDs showed stronger N doping, obvious blue shift of photoluminescence (PL) emission, and improved dispersion in organic system. After chloroform extraction and purification, the N-GQDs derived from citric acid-urea ethanol solution had the least loss and the most prominent optical performance (quantum yield =41.95%). The purified N-GQDs showed longer fluorescence lifetime than that before purification and still showed strong PL performance after compounding with ZnO ETL. N-GQDs modified ZnO ETL layer has stronger light transmittance and lower impedance. The stronger light transmittance is capable of improving the light absorption of the active layer while the lower impedance can promote electron transfer. Both properties are conductive to the PCE.

ce:section.ce:para: Polymer solar cells (PSCs) have unique advantages of light weight, low-temperature fabrication, suitable for mass production, and compatibility with flexible substrates. So far, the power conversion efficiency (PCE) of PSCs has not reached the threshold for commercial applications [1,2]. The limited PCE is caused by the lower charge mobility of the polymer, the short exciton diffusion length, the charge trapping in the collecting pathways and the insufficient light absorption. New strategies have been studied to alleviate the intrinsic limitations of polymer solar cells [3–5], in which the interface modification has a significant impact on the accelerating exciton dissociation and increasing light absorption without expanding the thickness of active layer and affecting the charge collection [6]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as an interface modifier, have unique optical properties and excellent electronic conductivity, and it can effectively prolong the lifetime of carriers and increase the quantum yield [7,8]. Zhang et al. [9] reported a general approach to tune the work function of GQD-modified electrodes using alkali metal cations. For ITO electrodes modified with these GQD-containing alkali metal cations, the work function can be finely tuned within the range of 4.0–4.5 eV, and the device performance is fairly comparable with that of ZnO used as the electron transport layer (ETL). Ding et al. [10] reported few-layered graphene quantum dots (F-GQDs) as an efficient hole transport layer (HTL). The small size and abundant COOH ensures film-forming ability and high work function. When used as HEL in PSC devices, F-GQDs outperform graphene oxide and PEDOT:PSS.

The optical and electrical properties of GQDs were readily tuned by controlling their chemical functionalities. Although there has been much interest in the design of functionalized GQDs, the purification of GQDs has not yet seen any significant progress [11–13]. Restricted by the preparation method, GQDs have problems, such as wide particle size distribution and high impurity content. However, the commonly used dialysis technology is complicated and time-consuming and limited by the pore size of the dialysis membrane itself. Thus, obtaining uniform GQDs is difficult. Moreover, the application of inhomogeneous GQDs in PSCs often shows uneven film formation and easy quenching, which limits the exertion of their photoelectric properties.

According to the purification idea of liquid-liquid extraction of protein, combined with the fact that N-GQDs have functional groups similar to protein, such as amino group and carboxyl group, it is proposed to separate the mixture by using different solubility, that is, using high stability salt to produce phase transformation separation in homogeneous mixed solution, and extract N-GQDs at the same time to further complete the purification more effectively [14]. Haeshin Lee et al. [15] demonstrated that salting out is a useful and scalable method for purifying graphene oxide (GO). At relatively low ammonium sulfate concentrations (~10 mM), large GO with predominant hydrophobic domains is precipitated. As the salt concentration increases, smaller and less GO with predominant hydrophobic domains is purified, and eventually GQDs are isolated.

The purification of GQDs can be completed by phase separation and extraction technology, and GQDs in organic solvents can be obtained. First, miscible organic solvent is added into the aqueous phase GQDs, then saturated inorganic salt solution is added gradually to salt it out into the organic solution, finally the required organic phase GQDs are separate. Therefore, the purification of GQDs and the transfer from aqueous to organic solution are completed.

This paper focuses on the purification of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) by salting-out extraction. With the increase in the concentration of salt solution, the large-size N-GQDs were salted-out gradually, and liquid–liquid was extracted at the same time. The changes in structure and optical properties of N-GQDs before and after purification were characterized and analyzed. Furthermore, N-GQDs before and after purification were introduced into the ZnO ETL layer of the inverted PSCs with the structure of ITO/ZnO/PTB7:PC71BM/MoO3/Al. Through the modification of ZnO ETL by N-GQDs, the electron transfer in ETL was improved, and then the efficiency of PSCs was improved.

Citric acid (CA), ammonia, urea, NaOH, N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF), tert-butanol, ethanol, acetone, hexadecyl trimethyl ammonium bromide, cyclohexane, tetrahydrofuran (THF), ammonium sulfate, chloroform, concentrated sulfuric acid, quinine sulfate, and dialysis bag (1000 Da) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Company or Beijing Chemical Reagent Company. The electron acceptor material PC71BM was purchased from Solarmer, whereas the electron donor material PTB7 was purchased from 1-material Chemscitech. All chemicals used in this work were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification. ITO glass (1 mm thickness, resistance < 10 Ω/sq) was purchased from Zhuhai Kaivo Optoelectronic Technology Co., Ltd. Deionized water was used for the preparation of aqueous solution in all of the experiments.
N-GQDs were prepared using hydrothermal/solvothermal methods with citric acid (CA) as carbon source. Various kinds of N-GQDs were obtained according to different nitrogen sources and solvents.

A-GQDs [16,17]: CA (1 g) and ammonia solution (10 mL) were mixed under vigorous stirring. The mixture was then transferred into a 25 mL autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 10 h. After cooling to room temperature, the mixture was centrifuged (8000 rpm, 10 min), and the supernatant was collected. The obtained supernatant was filtered with 220 nm PES microporous membrane to remove the remaining residue. A-GQDs were used to denote the obtained N-GQDs.

U-GQDs [18,19]: CA (0.21 g) and urea (0.6 g) were dissolved in 5 mL water, and the solution was transferred into a 25 mL autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 4 h. After cooling to room temperature, the mixture was centrifuged (8000 rpm, 10 min), and the supernatant was collected. The obtained supernatant was filtered with 220 nm PES microporous membrane to remove the remaining residue. U-GQDs were used to denote the obtained N-GQDs.

Ae-GQDs: CA, ammonia solution, and ethanol were mixed under vigorous stirring. The mixture undergoes a solvothermal process at 180 °C for 4 h. The remaining operations are the same as above. Ae-GQDs were used to denote the obtained N-GQDs.

Ue-GQDs: CA, urea, and ethanol were mixed under vigorous stirring. The mixture undergoes a solvothermal process at 180 °C for 4 h. The remaining operations are the same as stated above. Ue-GQDs were used to denote the obtained N-GQDs.
N-GQDs were purified in three different ways, and the detailed operations are presented as follows.

A-GQDs and U-GQDs solution were placed in a dialysis bag (1000 Da) by using deionized water as the dialysis solution under magnetic stirring, and the water was changed every 7 h until full dialysis for 3 days. The obtained N-GQDs solution was freeze-dried. A-GQDs and U-GQDs were named A-GQDs-DL and U-GQDs-DL after dialysis, respectively. A-GQDs-DL and U-GQDs-DL were redispersed in water to make a solution with certain concentration, and the solution was stored in the dark at 4 °C for later experiments.

THF (10 mL) was added to the A-GQDs-DL or U-GQDs-DL solution, and the mixture was transferred into an autoclave and heated for 2 h at 100 °C. Then, the saturated ammonium sulfate solution was added in batches to the solution to separate the solution into two phases. The solution was centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 5 min, and the process was repeated three times. THF containing N-GQDs was separated and then evaporated to obtain solid N-GQDs, which were named as A-GQDs-SE and U-GQDs-SE.

THF (10 mL) was added to the N-GQDs (Ae-GQDs and Ue-GQDs) solution, and the salting out extraction is the same as described above. Ae-GQDs and Ue-GQDs after salting out extraction were named Ae-GQDs-SE and Ue-GQDs-SE, respectively.

Chloroform (10 mL) was mixed into the N-GQDs (Ae-GQDs and Ue-GQDs) solution. The mixture was centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 5 min, and the process was repeated three times. Chloroform that contains N-GQDs was separated and then evaporated to obtain solid N-GQDs, which were named as Ae-GQDs-Os and Ue-GQDs-Os.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Modification of ZnO ETL by N-GQDs and photovoltaic device fabrication
ce:section.ce:para: ZnO ETL was deposited by spin-coating a ZnO sol onto cleaned ITO substrates at 4000 rpm for 30 s and annealed at 150 °C for 30 min. A total of 100 µL N-GQDs solution was dropped on ZnO film, spin coating was performed at 2000 rpm for 30 s, and then annealing was performed at 150 °C for 10 min. Afterwards, a mixed chlorobenzene solution of PTB7 and PC71BM (2:3 w/w) was spin-coated at 1400 rpm for 40 s onto ZnO/N-GQDs ETL and then annealed at 120 °C for 10 min under vacuum condition. Finally, a 10 nm MoO3 layer and a 100 nm aluminum cathode were deposited on the top of active layer under vacuum condition for a hole transport layer and a top anode, respectively. The device with single ZnO ETL is used as the control device.

The morphology and size of purified N-GQDs were characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM). The functional groups, structure, and component of N-GQDs were analyzed by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray diffraction (XRD), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The optical properties of purified N-GQDs were studied by ultraviolet-visible (UV-Vis) spectrophotometer and molecular fluorescence spectrometer. The current density–voltage (J–V) curves of the solar cells were measured using a Keithley 2401 under 1-sun illumination (AM 1.5, 100 mW/cm2). The external quantum efficiency (EQE) was measured by a solar cell QE/IPCE measurement system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of purification on the morphology and size of N-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 shows the TEM images of purified N-GQDs. The histograms in Fig. 1 show that the average diameter of N-GQDs for A-GQDs-DL, A-GQDs-SE, Ae-GQDs-SE, U-GQDs-DL, U-GQDs -SE, Ue-GQDs-SE is 18, 8, 12, 2, 6, and 7 nm, respectively. The purified N-GQDs showed good dispersion in the organic system. Fig. 2a shows the FTIR spectra of A-GQDs-SE, Ae-GQDs-SE, U-GQDs-SE, and Ue-GQDs-SE. Peaks at 3311, 2952, 3302, 2982, 3173, 2979, 3248, and 3039 cm−1 correspond to the tensile vibration of O–H and N–H [20]. The C = O tensile vibration at 1710 cm−1 is weakened, whereas the C–N tensile vibration at 1400 cm−1 is enhanced, indicating that more N is doped into GQDs after salting out extraction. Fig. 2b shows that the XRD patterns and the diffraction peaks are located at 21.5°, 27.6°, 27.2°, and 27.1°. The（002）layer spacing calculated according to Bragg equation are 4.13, 3.23, 3.28, and 3.29 Å for A-GQDs-SE, Ae-GQDs-SE, U-GQDs-SE, and Ue-GQDs-SE, respectively. Among them, A-GQDs-SE shows a larger layer spacing than that before purification (3.86 Å), indicating that nitrogen doping will be further exacerbated in the process of salting out extraction. Ae-GQDs-SE, U-GQDs-SE, and Ue-GQDs-SE showed reduced layer spacing, which can be attributed to the combination and dehydration of oxygen-containing functional groups in the purification process.

Table 1 lists the elemental compositions calculated from the XPS survey spectrum of U-GQDs, U-GQDs-DL, Ue-GQDs, and Ue-GQDs-SE (Fig. S1). The ratios of N and C contents (RN/C) of the four N-GQDs are 0.23, 0.35, 0.36, and 0.54. The value of RN/C increased compared with that before purification, indicating that dialysis and salting-out extraction could enhance the N-doping, especially for Ue-GQDs-SE. Fig. 3 shows the high-resolution fitting XPS spectra of C1s and N1s peaks of N-GQDs before and after purification [21,22]. C1s peaks in Fig. 3a and c are mainly related to sp2, sp3, and oxidized C peak, and the peak position and intensity are listed in Table 2. U-GQDs-DL shows more sp2 carbon content than U-GQDs, which means that carbon can be combined in the form of ring or double bond, thereby showing a higher degree of graphitization. Ue-GQDs-SE shows a slight increase in sp2 carbon content, and more oxidized carbon is reduced into saturated sp3 carbon. N1s peaks in Fig. 3a and c are divided into pyrrole N and graphite N by peak fitting, and their location and intensity are listed in Table 3. U-GQDs-DL contains more pyrrole N, whereas Ue-GQDs-SE contains more graphite N. Pyrrole N is a five-membered ring formed by the dehydration between adjacent carboxyl and amino groups, which in turn confirms the decrease in oxygen content in U-GQDs-DL. Ue-GQDs-SE shows more graphite N because of the bonding between N and the three adjacent C atoms. Therefore, after purification, the surface group, bonding mode, and nitrogen content of N-GQDs will change, which will affect their optical properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of purification on the optical properties of N-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 shows the PL emission of the purified N-GQDs at the excitation wavelength of 365 nm. The PL peak position of A-GQDs, A-GQDs-SE, Ae-GQDs-SE, Ae-GQDs-Os, and Ae-GQDs-SE are at 465, 440, 465, 455, and 459 nm, respectively (Fig. 4a). The A-GQDs-SE showed a blue shift with amplitude of approximately 25 nm. Ae-GQDs-Os and Ae-GQDs-SE, compared with Ae-GQDs, also show some blue shift, and their amplitudes are10 nm and 6 nm, respectively. Fig. 4b shows the PL spectra of U-GQDs, U-GQDs-SE, Ue-GQDs-Os, and Ue-GQDs-SE, which correspond to the peak positions at 470, 465, 460, 465, and 465 nm, respectively. Purified U-GQDs also showed some blue shift.

The UV-Vis, PLE, and PL spectra of N-GQDs at different excitation wavelengths are illustrated in Fig. 5. In Fig. 5a–d, the UV-Vis absorption peaks of A-GQDs, A-GQDs-se, U-GQDs and U-GQDS-SE are located at 332, 283, 335, and 284 nm, respectively, which correspond to the n→π * transitions between the surface state levels induced by surface functional groups. The optimal peak position of PLE in A-GQDs and U-GQDs is consistent with the UV-Vis absorption peaks at long wavelengths, and the PL fluorescence intensity obtained by taking this as the excitation wavelength is also the largest. The corresponding long-band UV-Vis absorption of A-GQDs-SE and U-GQDS-SE is very weak, that is, the purification has achieved a certain effect, and some functional groups have been removed. In addition, the purified sample shows a more fixed fluorescence emission peak position. Fig. 5e–j shows the optical properties of Ae-GQDs/ Ue-GQDs prepared by ethanol solvothermal and purified Ae-GQDs-SE, Ue-GQDs-SE Ae-GQDs-Os, and Ue-GQDs-Os. The UV-Vis absorption of Ue-GQDs showed a significant red shift of approximately 17 nm compared with Ae-GQDs. The purified samples showed obvious blue shift due to more graphitization structures and quantum confinement effects, especially Ae-GQDs-Os and Ue-GQDs-Os. Ae-GQDs and Ue-GQDs showed fixed emission peak positions of 465 nm and 460 nm, respectively, whereas the emission peak positions of the purified samples showed blue shift and excitation wavelength-dependent behavior.

Fig. 6 shows the UV-Vis absorption and PL fluorescence spectra of N-GQDs before and after purification. Table 4 lists the absorbance, PL integral area, and quantum yield (QY) of the corresponding N-GQDs. The QY of the N-GQDs is calculated using quinine sulfate as a reference. Among them, Ue-GQDs-Os has the most prominent optical performance and the least sample loss in purification. In the purification process, large particle size N-GQDs will be lost, the corresponding PL emission band will be blue shifted, the fluorescence intensity will be weakened, and the corresponding QY will be decreased, which is consistent with the data in Table 4. However, the particle size distribution, surface state, and dispersion in organic phase of purified N-GQD are more suitable for its application in polymer solar cells.

Fig. 7 shows the time resolved PL decay curves of N-GQDs. Those with longer fluorescence average life are A-GQDs-SE, Ue-GQDs-SE, and Ue-GQDs-Os. The N-GQDs purified by salting out extraction or organic extraction shows longer fluorescence life, which is more conducive to electron transfer and photoelectric conversion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of purification on photovoltaic performance
ce:section.ce:para: The photovoltaic parameters of devices with different N-GQD-modified ZnO ETLs are listed in Table 5. Compared with the control device, the PCE of N-GQD-modified devices is improved. After N-GQD modification, the short-circuit current density (J SC) shows some improvements, whereas the open circuit voltage (V OC) has slight changes because V OC is generally determined by the energy level difference between the PTB7 and PC71BM. In different N-GQDs, the performance of Ae-GQDs-Os and Ue-GQDs-SE is prominent, and J SC and the filling factor (FF) improve to a certain extent. The EQE of Ae-GQDs-Os and Ae-GQDs-SE-modified devices were measured further, and the curves were shown in Fig. 8. For the wavelength shorter than 515 nm, the Ae-GQDs-O-modified devices showed significantly higher EQE values than the control devices. Meanwhile, the Ae-GQDs-SE modified photovoltaic devices showed higher EQE values than the control devices at the wavelength shorter than 557 nm. Similarly, they all showed a significant increase in EQE at approximately 475 nm. The wavelength position of EQE increases corresponds to the PL emission band of N-GQDs. Therefore, the N-GQDs introduced as a modifier increase the utilization of light energy by the active layer. This phenomenon is also confirmed by the fluorescence state of N-GQDs on ZnO ETL and the enhancement of the light absorption of the active layer (Figs. S2 and 3). In addition to the use of PL to increase the light absorption of the active layer, N-GQD-modified ZnO ETL layer has lower impedance, which is conducive to electron transfer and in improving the PCE of photovoltaic devices (Fig. S4 and Table S1).

Purification is an important process in the preparation of GQDs, which can remove impurities, improve the uniformity of particle size, and transfer N-GQDs from aqueous phase to organic phase successfully. Purified N-GQDs showed stronger N doping, obvious blue shift of PL emission spectrum, and longer fluorescence lifetime. Furthermore, purified N-GQDs were demonstrated as an effective modifier of ZnO ETL of polymer solar cells with an ITO/ZnO/PTB7:PC71BM/MoO3/Al structure. The performance of the polymer solar cell is improved by increasing the light absorption of the active layer and accelerating the electron transfer. Among N-GQDs, the promotion effect of Ae-GQDs-Os to photovoltaic device is the most obvious.

Yanyun Zhu: Methodology, Validation, Writing – original draft. Chunai Dai: Formal analysis. Changshi Hao: Investigation, Resources. Hao Guo: Data curation, Resources. Luting Yan: Supervision, Conceptualization Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (11475017).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2022.129401.

.
